# UC-22.2.11 · NIS2 Cryptography and Encryption Policy Monitoring

## Description

Article 21(2)(h) requires policies and procedures for cryptography and encryption. This use case monitors TLS certificate health, identifies weak cipher usage, detects unencrypted protocols on NIS2-scoped networks, and tracks encryption-at-rest status — providing continuous evidence that cryptographic controls are operational and current.

## Value

Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIS2 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes network, vulnerability. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.

## Implementation

(1) Ingest TLS handshake metadata from Splunk Stream or proxy logs; (2) monitor for deprecated protocols (TLS 1.0/1.1, SSLv3) and weak ciphers (RC4, DES, 3DES, export ciphers); (3) track certificate expiry for NIS2-scoped services; (4) correlate vulnerability scanner findings for crypto weaknesses; (5) report encryption-at-rest coverage for databases and file stores. Indexes required: `index=network`, `index=vulnerability`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: CIM Certificate data model, `index=network` (TLS metadata), `index=vulnerability` (crypto-related findings).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Ingest TLS handshake metadata from Splunk Stream or proxy logs; (2) monitor for deprecated protocols (TLS 1.0/1.1, SSLv3) and weak ciphers (RC4, DES, 3DES, export ciphers); (3) track certificate expiry for NIS2-scoped services; (4) correlate vulnerability scanner findings for crypto weaknesses; (5) report encryption-at-rest coverage for databases and file stores.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates
  where All_Certificates.ssl_end_time < relative_time(now(), "+30d")
  by All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name All_Certificates.ssl_end_time All_Certificates.ssl_issuer_common_name
| rename All_Certificates.* as *
| eval days_until_expiry=round((ssl_end_time - now())/86400, 0)
| eval status=case(days_until_expiry < 0, "EXPIRED", days_until_expiry < 14, "CRITICAL", days_until_expiry < 30, "WARNING")
| sort days_until_expiry
| table ssl_subject_common_name, ssl_issuer_common_name, days_until_expiry, status
```

Understanding this SPL

**NIS2 Cryptography and Encryption Policy Monitoring (Art. 21(2)(h))** — Article 21(2)(h) requires policies and procedures for cryptography and encryption. This use case monitors TLS certificate health, identifies weak cipher usage, detects unencrypted protocols on NIS2-scoped networks, and tracks encryption-at-rest status — providing continuous evidence that cryptographic controls are operational and current.

Documented **Data sources**: CIM Certificate data model, `index=network` (TLS metadata), `index=vulnerability` (crypto-related findings). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Stream (Splunkbase 1809), Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Certificates.All_Certificates` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **days_until_expiry** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **status** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

This use case does not use a CIM tstats shortcut; the primary SPL above is the source of truth after field normalization in your environment.

Output fields: days_until_expiry, ssl_issuer_common_name, ssl_subject_common_name, status.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (expiring/expired certificates), Pie chart (TLS version distribution), Bar chart (weak ciphers by host), Single value (% services using TLS 1.2+).

## SPL

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Certificates.All_Certificates
  where All_Certificates.ssl_end_time < relative_time(now(), "+30d")
  by All_Certificates.ssl_subject_common_name All_Certificates.ssl_end_time All_Certificates.ssl_issuer_common_name
| rename All_Certificates.* as *
| eval days_until_expiry=round((ssl_end_time - now())/86400, 0)
| eval status=case(days_until_expiry < 0, "EXPIRED", days_until_expiry < 14, "CRITICAL", days_until_expiry < 30, "WARNING")
| sort days_until_expiry
| table ssl_subject_common_name, ssl_issuer_common_name, days_until_expiry, status
```

## Visualization

Table (expiring/expired certificates), Pie chart (TLS version distribution), Bar chart (weak ciphers by host), Single value (% services using TLS 1.2+). Key fields: `days_until_expiry`, `ssl_issuer_common_name`, `ssl_subject_common_name`, `status`.
